coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive enantioselective spectrofluorimetric determination of R-/S-mandelic acid using l-tryptophan-modified amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots as novel high-throughput chiral nanoprobes
coredata.dc:creator: Dehghani, Zahra
Akhond, Morteza
Hormozi Jangi, Saeed Reza
Absalan, Ghodratollah
coredata.dc:description: Amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots (NH2–SiO2-CDs) were covalently modified by l-tryptophan (chiral selector) by producing an amide bond between carboxyl groups of L-try and amino groups of NH2–SiO2-CDs to develop a novel high throughput chiral nanoprobes (L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs) for highly sensitive and enantioselective quantification of S-/R-mandelic acid (S-/R-Man). The method showed a great difference between S- and R-isomers (enantioselectivity coefficient = 4.17) due to the ultra-stability of the Meisenheimer complex that was formed between S-isomer and nanoprobe (KS-Man/KR-man = 2122.7, where K is the binding-constant). At optimal experimental conditions, two linear ranges of 0.5–25.0 (LOD of 0.05 μM) and 0.5–22.0 μM (LOD of 0.27 μM) for S- and R-Man, respectively, along with an enhanced sensitivity toward S-isomer (about 5.7-fold higher than R-isomer) were attained. High selectivity for the determination of mandelic acid was achieved compared to metal ions, amino acids, and sugars that commonly coexist with it. Intra-day as well as inter-day assays, respectively, showed RSD values of about 3.2 and 3.9%. The mechanistic studies were performed for proving the enantioselective behavior of the developed nanoprobe. The method was then used for S-/R-mandelic acid determination in bio-samples. The figures of merit for the method were found to be better than those already reported for enantioselective detection of R-/S-Man.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the fluorescent enantioselective nanoprobe
Synthesis of l-tryptophan modified amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots (L-try–CONH–sio2-cds)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Optical photographs of as-prepared nanomaterials under visible light and UV-lamp (1: N-doped carbon dots; 2: SiO2-CDs; 3: NH2–SiO2-CDs; 4: L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs) and (B) FL spectra of N-doped carbon dots, SiO2-CDs, NH2–SiO2-CDs, and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs.

(A) FT-IR spectra of SiO2-CDs, NH2–SiO2-CDs, and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs, (B) XRD pattern of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs, (C) TEM image of N-doped CDs, as well as SEM images of (D) SiO2-CDs, (E) NH2–SiO2-CDs, and (F) L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs.
(A) Enantioselectivity of as-prepared nanoprobe toward 10.0 μM of S-/R-mandelic acid and (B) proposed mechanism for enantioselective determination of S-/R-mandelic acid using L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs.
(A) FL spectra of L-try–CON–SiO2-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of S-mandelic acid, (B) its FL spectra in the presence of R-mandelic acid, and (C) corresponding calibration curves.
(A) Selectivity of the developed nanosensor and (B) calibration of a racemic mixture of mandelic acid enantiomers (inset: corresponding FL spectra).
S-mandelic acid determination in human serum samples.
Comparison of figures of merit of developed method with reported ones for R- and S-mandelic acid enantioselective determination.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive enantioselective spectrofluorimetric determination of R-/S-mandelic acid using l-tryptophan-modified amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots as novel high-throughput chiral nanoprobes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Massoumi Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 71454, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Professor Massoumi Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots (NH2–SiO2-CDs) were covalently modified by l-tryptophan (chiral selector) by producing an amide bond between carboxyl groups of L-try and amino groups of NH2–SiO2-CDs to develop a novel high throughput chiral nanoprobes (L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs) for highly sensitive and enantioselective quantification of S-/R-mandelic acid (S-/R-Man). The method showed a great difference between S- and R-isomers (enantioselectivity coefficient = 4.17) due to the ultra-stability of the Meisenheimer complex that was formed between S-isomer and nanoprobe (KS-Man/KR-man = 2122.7, where K is the binding-constant). At optimal experimental conditions, two linear ranges of 0.5–25.0 (LOD of 0.05 μM) and 0.5–22.0 μM (LOD of 0.27 μM) for S- and R-Man, respectively, along with an enhanced sensitivity toward S-isomer (about 5.7-fold higher than R-isomer) were attained. High selectivity for the determination of mandelic acid was achieved compared to metal ions, amino acids, and sugars that commonly coexist with it. Intra-day as well as inter-day assays, respectively, showed RSD values of about 3.2 and 3.9%. The mechanistic studies were performed for proving the enantioselective behavior of the developed nanoprobe. The method was then used for S-/R-mandelic acid determination in bio-samples. The figures of merit for the method were found to be better than those already reported for enantioselective detection of R-/S-Man.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The presented data in the manuscript and supporting information are sufficient for representation of the developed work.

ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of chemicals is found to exist as chiral compounds. This includes several types of carbocyclic acids, alkaloids, amino acids, proteins, enzymes, hormones carbohydrates, and nucleosides [1,2]. Due to the different properties of R-/S- enantiomers and their diverse effects on the human body, the chiral recognition/quantification of these compounds is the most attractive research field. Up to now, several analytical methods, for instance, LCMS (liquid chromatography hyphenated with mass spectrometry [3], gas chromatography [4–6], LCESI-MS-MS (liquid chromatography hyphenated with electrospray ionization tandem mass spectroscopy) [7], HPLC (high-performance liquid chromatography) [8–10], spectrophotometry [11–14], electrochemistry [15,16], electrophoresis [17], and microbalance sensors [18] have been used for the chiral recognition/quantification of chiral compounds. However, most of these methods are expensive, time-consuming, and require expert operators. Moreover, some of these methods despite their simplicity and cost-efficiency are poor in selectivity against other analog or co-existing compounds along with low sensitivity and poor enantioselectivity coefficients. Hence, developing a simple, cost-efficient, sensitive, and efficient method for the quantification of chiral compounds with a good enantioselectivity coefficient is desirable.

Fluorescence-based nanosensors are considered good candidates for being used in designing chiroptical sensing systems for chiral resolution and trace analysis of enantiomers because of their excellent selectivity, enantioselectivity coefficient, and sensitivity. Moreover, their corresponding methodology is simple and cost-effective [19–23]. A wide variety of fluorescent nanomaterials especially carbon dots (CDs) and quantum dots (QDs) are fascinated to construct high throughput nanosensors toward chiral proposes. It should be mentioned that the function of the developed enantioselective nanosensors, rather than CDs- or QDs-based nanosensors, is based on either fluorescence quenching or fluorescence-enhancing mechanism. This has been reported for some analytical probes when they were in contact with a corresponding analyte and showed a significant difference between R- and S- enantiomers [24–29].

Most QDs, despite their unique advantages such as high quantum yield, high sensitivity, good photostability, narrow emission, and broad excitation bands, are very toxic with high cost and some of them are insoluble in water and their synthesis in organic media limit their practical safe applications [24–29]. Hence, to reach a green and efficient nanosensor, carbon dots have been widely used with different strategies in designing nanosensors and enantioselective methods for the determination and chiral recognition of different analytes due to their unique strengths and properties. These include low toxicity, high fluorescent emission, excellent water solubility, robust chemical inertness, excellent photo-stability, lack of blinking, easy fabrication, excellent biocompatibility, and low cost [30–41].

The studies have shown that, by chemical heteroatom doping, bandgap, electronic density of CDs and their quantum yield can be significantly improved [42,43]. The most popular method for improving the characteristics of CDs, such as quantum yield and high photoluminescence, is N-doping (N-CDs) [44–46]. The N-dopant is able to insert electrons into carbon-based materials, thus changing their electronic and photoluminescence properties and making them favorable in the field of fluorescence-based sensors and biosensors [47].

The quantification and chiral recognition of mandelic acid (Man), an alpha-hydroxy acid derived from bitter almonds, are of great concern due to its application in manufacturing health care products, for instance, use in chemical skin peels, as a urinary antiseptic and as a component of other medicines) [48]. Besides, irregular pigmentation, photo-aging, and antimicrobials have been studied and widely considered [49,50]. Unfortunately, mandelic acid in the vicinity of light, heat, and base is very unstable and decomposes into biologically inactive compounds [51]. A high level of mandelic acid, for instance, causes irritating to the skin and allergic reactions. For physiological functions, therefore, the human body needs only a little amount of this acid [52]. Based on these considerations, sensitive, selective, repeatable, and accurate trace quantification and chiral recognition of mandelic acid in biological samples are very important to save human health. Notably, up to now, several important works were also reported on chiral recognition of mandelic acid enantiomers. For instance, quartz crystal microbalance modified by l-phenylalanine was used for this aim by Guo et al., 2009 [53]. In addition, enantioselective recognition of mandelic acid by fluorescent sensors was also reported by Xu et al. [54]. Besides, l-tartaric acid-capped gold nanoparticles were utilized for visual chiral sensing of mandelic acid enantiomers by Song et al., in 2015 [55]. Moreover, enantioselective electrochemical sensing of mandelic acid enantiomers was reported by Borazjan et al. (2017) [56]. In fact, the design of a nanoprobe with high enantioselectivity is a key factor for developing high-throughput mandelic acid sensors.

In this work, amino-functional N-doped CDs (NH2–SiO2-CDs) with bright blue luminescence were covalently modified by l-tryptophan as chiral selector via an amide bond formation between CDs and chiral selector to produce a novel high sensitive, selective, and accurate chiral nanosensor (L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs) with excellent enantioselectivity coefficient toward enantioselective determination of R-/S-mandelic acid in human blood serum. The effective experimental factors on the enantioselective quantification of mandelic acid enantiomers (i.e., S- and R-Man) such as buffer concentration and its type, as well as sensor response time were evaluated and optimized. The mechanism behind the enantioselectivity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was studied for proving the observed behavior of the developed nanoprobe. It is notable that, at the same time, optically active mandelic acid with good biodegradability is used as an important intermediate in the synthesis of various chiral drugs [57]. R-mandelic acid could be used as a side chain modifier of benzyl tetracosporin, S-mandelic acid is a kind of synthetic of S Bunin drugs for the treatment of urgency, frequency of urination, and urinary incontinence [58]. In addition, chiral mandelic acid is also a kind of important racemic resolution reagent [59]. Notably, the developed method is simpler and faster than many reported methods along with high accuracy, sensitivity, and selectivity.

R-mandelic acid (R-Man), S- mandelic acid (S-Man), citric acid, urea, ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), l-tryptophan (L-Try), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid (HOAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), sodium citrate, tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethan (Tris), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), ammonia (NH3), l-serine (L-Ser), l-histidine (L-His), l-glycine (L-Gly), l-leucine (L-Leu), l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-glutamine (L-Glu), l-asparagine (L-Asp), l-phenylalanine (L-Phe), glucose, l-arabinose (L-Ara), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), copper acetate, iron (II) chloride, and calcium nitrate, and zinc acetate were from Merck chemical company. Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide) DCC (and deionized water (DW) were from Sigma-Aldrich Company and Zolal Teb Shimico Company, respectively. The reaction starter solution was prepared through using NH4F (300 mg), hydroxyl ammonium chloride (300 mg), NH3 (3.0 mL, 25%), and deionized water (11.0 mL) according to the literature [30,60,61].

A thermostat-equipped PerkinElmer LS50B spectrofluorometer was used for spectrofluorimetric measurement and optical characterization as well as for the FL evaluations. A Metrohm pH meter (model: 780) was utilized for pH measurements. The UV–Visible spectra were recorded by an Ultrospec 4000 spectrophotometer (United Kingdom; Pharmacia Biotech). The IR spectra were attained by using an FT-IR spectrometer (model: spectrum RXI, from PerkinElmer). The SEM (scanning electron microscopy) was carried out by a TESCAN-Vega 3 instrument (TESCAN company, Czech Republic). The size of the as-prepared unmodified CDs was evaluated by recording their TEM (transmission electron microscopy) images using a corresponding instrument provided from Zeiss company (model EL10C working under an accelerating voltage of 80 kV). Moreover, The EDX spectra were recorded using an electronic microscope from MIRA3-TESCAN company (15 KV, www.tescan.com). The crystal structures of the compounds were calculated through XRD analysis using an XRD instrument from Rigaku D/max-3C company (www.Rigaku.Com/en, Japan) working at the Cu-Kα radiation line (1.54 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the fluorescent enantioselective nanoprobe

ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of N- doped CDs was performed using a simple hydrothermal method according to the literature [62]. To do this, initially, 2.2 g of citric acid and 1.8 g urea were added to 20.0 mL of deionized water, and the solution was sonicated for 10 min to obtain a homogenous solution. Afterward, it was transferred to an autoclave at 180.0 ± 1.0 °C for 4 h to form dark green N-doped CDs. After cooling, the as-prepared CDs were centrifuged for 30.0 min (speed: 3000 rpm) to remove the insoluble species and store the product at 4 ± 1 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of l-tryptophan modified amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots (L-try–CONH–sio2-cds)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped CDs were silica-coated (SiO2-CDs) by TEOS. To do this, N- doped CDs (5.0 mL) were added to a solution containing 25.0 mL EtOH and 5.0 mL deionized water and sonicated for 5 min. The synthesis was followed by adding 456 μL (2.0 mmol) of TEOS, performing a 5.0-min sonication, and then introducing 200 μL of the reaction starter. The resulted mixture was degassed with nitrogen for 1.0 min and stirred for 5.0 h at ambient conditions. Finally, the SiO2-CDs were dried at 37 ± 1 °C. In the second step, amino-functional N-doped silica-coated carbon dots (NH2–SiO2-CDs) were synthesized by adding 200 mg of SiO2-CDs to 25.0 mL EtOH and deionized water (5.0 mL) followed by sonication for 10 min. An aliquot of 356 μL of APTES was added and then the mixture was sonicated for 5.0 min. Adding 200 μL of the reaction starter (the reaction starter is a solution containing 300 mg NH4F, 300 mg of hydroxyl ammonium chloride, 3.0 mL of 25% NH3, and 11.0 mL of deionized water, prepared based on the reported literature [30,60,61]), degassing with nitrogen for 1.0 min, stirring for 2.0 h at the ambient temperature, and then refluxing for 5.0 h at 40.0 ± 1.0 °C were the next processes that were done in order. The resulting NH2–SiO2-CDs were dried at 37 ± 1 °C. For the synthesis of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs, 100.0 mg l-tryptophan and 200.0 mg DCC (as the carboxyl group activator for the production of amide bond [60]) were added to 25.0 MeOH and sonicated for 10 min, then 500 mg of NH2–SiO2-CDs was added and the resulted mixture was refluxed for 24 h at 40 ± 1 °C. The resulting product was dried at 37 ± 1 °C. After that, 75 mg of the resulting L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was dispersed into 150.0 mL deionized water to be stored at a temperature of 4 ± 1 °C and then used whenever required. The final pH of the suspension was about 6.52.

For each experiment, initially, 500 μL of the stock solution of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs (calculated to be 0.5.0 mg L−1 in the final solution) was added to 1.3 mL of the citrate buffer (0.05 M with a pH of 6.0). Then, 200 μL of R- or S-mandelic acid sample solution with different concentrations was introduced into the mixture, followed by mixing at the ambient temperature for 1.0 min. Afterward, the fluorescence measurements were performed over the range of 400.0–600.0 nm (with a λ max at 452.0 nm) under an excitation wavelength of 350.0 nm. The optimization of effective factors on both enantioselectivity and sensitivity of the method was done by probing the F0/F ratio where F and F0, respectively, were the intensities of the fluorescence signal of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs in the presence and absence of mandelic acid enantiomers. The analytical response, Δ (F0/F), was defined as the difference between the values of F0/F for S- and R-mandelic acids.

The performance of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was examined for S-Man determination in human blood serum samples using the spiked-recovery experiments. To do this, the human serum samples were obtained from Namazi Hospital (Shiraz, Iran) and then used for analysis using the developed method toward the determination of S-Man upon 4.0-fold dilution without any deproteinization. To do the spiked-recovery experiments, certain concentrations of S-Man in serum samples, followed by incubation for 24 h at 4 °C. Afterward, the concentration of the spiked S-Man was calculated using the nanoprobe via FL measurements.

Initially, the appearance and physical state of the as-prepared materials were considered. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the N-doped carbon dots were transparent liquids with a green appearance. While after the silica coating with TEOS, the powdered SiO2-CDs with a deep green color were obtained. The color of silica-coated N-doped carbon dots was changed from deep green to pistachio-green after its modification with APTES to produce NH2–SiO2-CDs. The developed chiral nanoprobe i.e., L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs, was synthesized by covalent modification of NH2–SiO2-CDs with L-try. After the amide bond formation between L-try and NH2–SiO2-CDs, the color was changed from pistachio-green to light blue. In-water dispersion of the nanomaterials was excellent and stable. It should be mentioned under the UV light, all the above-mentioned nanomaterials showed a strong blue emission. Hence, the optical fluorescent behavior of the as-synthesized nanomaterials was examined by FL spectroscopy. The FL spectra of N-doped CDs, SiO2-CDs, NH2–SiO2-CDs, and L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs are presented in Fig. 1B. As demonstrated, the N-doped CDs showed a semi-symmetric emission spectrum over 400.0–550.0 nm with a maximum absorbance at 452.0 nm while the excitation wavelength was 350.0 nm. Also, SiO2-CDs, NH2–SiO2-CDs, and L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs revealed a λ max of 452.0 nm which was the same as N-doped CDs with no considerable shift in either wavelength or its intensity. This revealed that repetitive modifications did not significantly affect the FL performance of N-doped CDs.

The absorption behavior of the as-prepared nanomaterials was investigated by UV–visible spectroscopy. Regarding Fig. S1A, the N-doped CDs showed two UV–Visible peaks over 330–440 nm and 450–800 nm centered at 389 nm and 600 nm, in order. After silica-coating, the product revealed a sharper UV–visible spectrum with two peaks with λ max at 350 nm and 612 nm (Fig. S1B). The evaluation of the absorption behavior of NH2–SiO2-CDs showed that the peak of CDs was changed from a semi-symmetrical spectrum in SiO2-CDs (Fig. S1B) to an asymmetrical peak after its modification with APTES (Fig. S1C). Moreover, the λ max was red-shifted from 350 nm to 612 nm in SiO2-CDs to 370 nm and 657 nm in NH2–SiO2-CDs, indicating successful amino-functionalization of SiO2-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1D, the UV–Visible band of NH2–SiO2-CDs was changed from an asymmetrical peak (Fig. S1C) to a shoulder band after its covalent modification with L-try (i.e., L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs; Fig. S1D).

The structural properties of the as-prepared nanomaterials were characterized through FT-IR, XRD as well as EDX examinations. The FT-IR analysis was performed to shine the successful functionalization of the as-synthesized N-doped CDs by recording the corresponding spectra of SiO2-CDs, NH2–SiO2-CDs, and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs as shown in Fig. 2 A. For the N-doped CDs, after its silica coating, the vibrations at 3434.61 and 801.69 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching of the –O–H and vibration of Si–OH in the SiO2-CDs, respectively [60,61,63,64]. The presence of a strong vibration at 1096.24 cm−1 shined the presence of Si–O–Si bond in SiO2-CDs (spectrum a) [30,60,65], revealing successful silica coating of N-doped CDs during the modification process [30,60,61,63–65]. After that, the amino-functionalization of SiO2-CDs was demanded using APTES, and the IR spectrum of NH2–SiO2-CDs was indexed as spectrum b showing broadband around 1601 cm−1 that can be assigned to the N–H bending of a primary amine [66]. The vibration bond at 3432 cm−1 (intense peak) was ascribed to the –NH2 group of APTES [60]. In addition, the peaks at 1355, 2924, 1095.18, and 784.85 cm−1 were recognized as the –C−H rocks of APTES [67], the –CH2 strength of APTES [67], Si–O–Si bond in SiO2 [30,60,65], and Si–OH bond [60,61,63,64], respectively. Hence, concerning the FT-IR results, it can be concluded that the amino-functionalization of SiO2-CDs using APTES was successfully performed upon the used method. Finally, the NH2–SiO2-CDs were modified by l-tryptophan via amide bond formation between the –NH2 groups in NH2–SiO2-CDs and –COOH groups in l-tryptophan using DCC as the carboxyl group activator [60]. The IR examination was done to prove the presence of an amide bond in the L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs structure after the reaction (spectrum d). The IR vibrations at 3454 (medium peak), 1077, 1391, 1604, and 1802 cm−1 were ascribed to the N–H stretching of the secondary amides, C–O of the carboxylic acids, C–O bonds of the amides, -C-N bond, and CO strength, respectively [30,60,61,63,65,68]. It is notable that after the formation of the covalent bond between the as-prepared NH2–SiO2-CDs and L-try, the intensity of the characteristic peak of N–H bending of primary amines (-NH2) at 1600 cm−1 was significantly decreased (about 54.3%) and broadened, pointing the participation of –NH2 for binding with the carboxyl group of L-try. Besides, the vibrational peak at about 1340 cm−1 (related to the -C-N stretch group of amines [67,69], see the spectrum of NH2–SiO2-CDs) disappeared after binding the carboxyl groups of L-try to the amino group of NH2–SiO2-CDs (see, FT-IR peak of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs), revealing the successful formation of –CONH- bond between L-try and NH2–SiO2-CDs during the reaction. Moreover, the peak of -N-H wag of primary amines [69,70] was shifted from 780 cm−1 in NH2–SiO2-CDs to 796 cm−1 in L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs along with the significant decrease in its intensity which can be assigned to the formation of an amide bond between L-try and amino groups of NH2–SiO2-CDs. In addition, the intense peak of amino groups at 3432 cm−1 in NH2–SiO2-CDs was broadened and shifted to 3454 cm−1 after the formation of binding of L-try and NH2–SiO2-CDs along with an appearance in medium intensity which is related to the presence of a secondary amide bond in structure of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs [30,60,61,63,65,68]. Moreover, the appearance of the IR vibrations at 2129 cm−1 and 1391 cm−1 related to the vibrations of secondary amides after bonding of L-try with NH2–SiO2-CDs, revealed that the formation of the amide bond was successful. Based on the IR spectrum of the L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs, it can be deduced that the amide bond was performed and the NH2–SiO2-CDs were successfully modified with l-tryptophan as the chiral selector. To prove the FT-IR results, the salt-induced desorption protocol was performed [71,72] for desorbing the non-covalent L-try from the surface of NH2–SiO2-CDs to prove the covalent nature of binding between L-try and NH2–SiO2-CDs, to do this, 5 mg of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was incubated with 1.0 M NaCl solution for 2 h to desorb the surface-adsorbed L-try (non-covalent) from the surface of nanoprobe, after 2 h, the concentration of desorbed L-try was calculated by analyzing the supernatant via a UV–Visible spectrophotometer, based on the literature [71]. The results revealed that about 3.2 ± 0.1% L-try was desorbed from the surface of the nanoprobe, proving the successful formation of amide bond during the synthesis process [71].”

Moreover, the EDX analysis was utilized for studying the elemental composition of as-prepared nanomaterials and proving the successful synthesis of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs via the proposed manner. The EDX patterns were recorded before and after silica-coating of N-doped CDs with TEOS; the results are brought in Figs. S2A and S2B, respectively. The characteristic lines of N-doped CDs include N, O, and C lines (Fig. S2A) while after its modification with TEOS (Fig. S2B), the new line of Si was appeared in the pattern, showing the successful silica-coating of CDs during the modification process. To obtain proper functional groups on the SiO2-CDs surface, for future modifications, the as-prepared SiO2-CDs were functionalized with APTES to have amino groups on the SiO2-CDs surface. The EDX results are provided in Fig. S2C. The characteristic lines of N, O, C, and Si are observable in this figure. Concerning the semi-quantitative results of EDX (Table S1) of SiO2-CDs and NH2–SiO2-CDs, the weight percentage of N was increased from 6.72% in SiO2-CDs to 10.22% in NH2–SiO2-CDs, revealing successful amino-coating of SiO2-CDs using APTES. Afterward, the modification of NH2–SiO2-CDs was performed using l-tryptophan. The EDX patterns of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs are presented in Fig. S2D. The typical peaks of N, O, C, and Si for L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs, and the weight percentage of N increased from 10.22% in NH2–SiO2-CDs to 10.87% in L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs (Table S1) due to the presence of l-tryptophan on the surface of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs. This revealed the successful modification of NH2–SiO2-CDs using l-tryptophan.

Finally, the XRD analysis of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was performed for examination of its crystalline structure (Fig. 2B). Fig. 1B presents the broad diffraction peaks around 13.15, 31.5, and 41.5° which were associated with the C (1 0 0), C (2 1 0), and C (3 0 0), respectively. It should be mentioned that the broad and low intense peaks of the as-prepared L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs shined its amorphous structure [73].

Regarding the N-doped CDs, TEM imaging was utilized to investigate their size and morphology (Fig. 2C). The as-prepared CDs were found to be spherical and have a size distribution of 4–12 nm with a mean size of about 6 nm. The morphologies of SiO2-CDs, NH2–SiO2-CDs, and L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs were tested by the SEM imaging method. The results in Fig. 2D, E, and 2F show that the SiO2-CDs were uniform and had spherical morphology with a mean size of almost 75 nm. The NH2–SiO2-CDs and L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs also showed semi-spherical morphologies with mean sizes of about 149 and 193 nm, respectively.

According to the FT-IR results, the as-prepared nanoprobe contains free amino groups (attributed to l-tryptophan) which can be used as the chiral functional groups for enantioselective detection of chiral compounds. Hence, the enantioselectivity of as-prepared L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was studied by chiral detection of 10.0 μM S-/R-mandelic acid enantiomers. This was performed based on the fluorescence quenching of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs by R-/S-Man via FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer); energy transfer from the negatively-charged carboxyl groups of mandelic acid to the positively-charged amino groups of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs (section 3.2) [65,74,75]. The method showed a great difference between S- and R-isomers thanks to the formation of an ultra-stable Meisenheimer complex between S-isomer (not for R-isomer) and nanoprobe (KS-Man/KR-man = 2122.7, K = binding constant (see section 3.2. Mechanistic studies). The fluorescence spectra of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs in both the presence and absence of 10.0 μM of individual enantiomer are presented in Fig. 3 A. According to this figure, although the fluorescence intensity of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs was quenched in the presence of both R and S enantiomers, the quenching intensity in the presence of S-enantiomer was significantly different. This verified the ability of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs for the detection of S- and R-mandelic acid enantiomers. It should be mentioned that the enantioselectivity coefficient (α) of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was calculated as high as 4.17 using α=(F0-Fs)/(F0-FR) [39] where F0, FR, and Fs are the FL intensity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs, respectively, in the absence of Man, in the presence of R-Man, and the presence of S-Man. The high value of the enantioselectivity coefficient reveals the high ability of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs for constructing a highly enantio-selective nanosensor toward mandelic acid determination. To confirm, the chiral recognition, the D-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs were also synthesized using the reported method in section 2.3.2, and its enantioselectivity was checked toward 10.0 μM R-/S-mandelic acid chiral detection by considering the enantioselectivity coefficient as an index for proving the chiral recognition of S- and R-mandelic acid. The corresponding spectra of D-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs in the presence and the absence of S-/R-mandelic acid as well as the enantioselectivity performances of D-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs compared to L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs were shown in Figure S3. The results revealed that although the L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs showed a significant quenching toward S-mandelic acid with a significant difference between S- (F0/F = 1.69) and R-isomers (F0/F = 1.11), the as-prepared D-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs revealed a significant quenching toward R-mandelic acid (F0/F = 1.44) rather than S-isomer (F0/F = 1.09). The enantioselectivity coefficient as an index for the ability of the as-prepared D-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs for application in chiral recognition of S-/R-mandelic acid was calculated using α=(F0-FR)/(F0-FS) [39], revealing an enantioselectivity coefficient at about α = 3.65 which is lower than that of the L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs (α = 4.17). Besides, considering this fact that d-tryptophan is 3.5-fold more expensive than L-try (the price of L-try-T9753-25G) is 78 USD while the price of D-try-T9753-25G is 284 USD, 98% HPLC, Sigma Aldrich), hence, utilizing D-try as the enantioselector cannot be a good choice for an economical point of view. Hence, considering both the enantioselectivity coefficient and economic considerations, l-tryptophan was selected as the enantioselective agent for the preparation of the enantioselective nanoprobe.

The mechanistic investigations were used in response to this question that why L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs revealed higher enantioselectivity toward S-mandelic acid than R-isomer. As reported in the literature, several parameters, for instance, complexation, charge transfer (FRET), transition type, the effect of conjugation, hydrogen bond formation, and electrostatic interactions (e.g., π-π interactions) may affect the fluorescence intensity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs [39,76,]. As discussed in 3.1.2, the L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs have amino groups on their surface which are related to the chiral selector (L-try), which can interact with a chiral molecule such as mandelic acid via homo- or hetero-chiral interaction [39]. Moreover, the chiral selector used in this study has a heterocycle containing nitrogen which can interact with the –OH or carbonyl (CO) group of mandelic acid and form a hydrogen bond. Also, the π-π interactions between the aromatic ring of R-/S- mandelic acid and the aromatic ring of L-try can considerably affect the FL intensity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs [30]. Hence, the developed L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs are an excellent choice for chiral sensing and detection with high enantioselectivity.

For the investigation of this proposal, the fluorescence spectra of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs in the absence, as well as the presence of both the R- Man and S-Man, were recorded (Fig. 3A). According to this figure, although the fluorescence intensity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was quenched by both enantiomers, the intensity of quenching by the S-enantiomer was significantly different that certified the ability of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs as an enantioselective fluorescent nanoprobe. The quenching by S-mandelic acid is considerably higher than its R-isomer probably because of the presence of strong stereospecific interactions developed between L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and S-mandelic isomer. The rational reason for this quenching process must be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) through transferring of energy from carboxyl groups in mandelic acid to amino groups in L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs based on a report in the literature [65,74,75]. More precisely, the optimization of pH exhibited that pH = 6.0 is the best for both enantioselectivity and analytical sensitivity of the L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs toward mandelic acid enantiomers (section 3.3). As reported in the literature, at the optimal conditions of this sensor (i.e., pH = 6.0, mandelic acid has a net charge equal to −1 [77] which points to the presence of negatively charged carboxyl groups in its structure, hence, the mandelic acid can act as an energy donor in FRET process [39]. On the other hand, mandelic acid contains a hydroxyl group that is very close to its carboxylic group, thus strong intramolecular hydrogen bonds are formed between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [78]. As the pH of the detection media increases, the intramolecular hydrogen bond force would weaken and consequently disappeared [79], which might improve the hydrogen bonding and energy donor-acceptor type of interactions between mandelic acid and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs, and thus give rise to great quenching in FL intensity. Moreover, the amino group of L-try presented on the surface of the analytical probe is protonated in acidic pH (optimum pH for the sensor) [80] which can accept the energy from negatively charged mandelic acid [30]. Hence, the energy transfer from mandelic acid to L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs leads to a complex formation (mandelic acid- L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs complex) and consequently, a considerable quenching in FL intensity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs occurs [1–3]. Moreover, the N hetero-atom presented in the L-try structure can also form an H-bond with the hydroxyl group of mandelic acid, causing a quenching in the FL intensity of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs [81].

Another way to prove the formation of a complex between L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and mandelic acid is to consider the apparent dissociation constant (i.e., pKa) of mandelic acid and the chiral selector. This helps to specify the surface charges at the detection conditions. The chiral selector (i.e., tryptophan) is a stable zwitterion at physiological conditions, which means that at these conditions the interactions between the mandelic acid enantiomers and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs occur via the neutral zwitterion form. Based on the pKa values of L-try (i.e., pKa (COO-) = 2.38 and pKa (-NH2) = 9.39) [80]. Besides, concerning this fact that the molecule is negatively charged at a pH higher than the pKa and has positively charged at a lower pH than the pKa [82], at pH = 6.0, the amino group of the L-try is protonated (i.e., –NH3 +) and can act as an energy acceptor. Considering the fact that the carboxyl group of L-try is responsible for amide group formation between amino-functional CDs and L-try [60], the protonated amino group of the chiral selector is then available for chiral analytes. Moreover, the pKa of mandelic acid is pKa = 3.75 [83], revealing the deprotonation of the carboxyl group at pH = 6.0 (optimal conditions). Consequently, one may conclude that the one of the main reasons for FL quenching in the presence of mandelic acid is the formation of a Meisenheimer complex between the carboxylic groups in mandelic acid and amino groups in L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs, as previously reported [1–3,8–10]. To test the complexation between mandelic acid and L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs, the temperature-dependent quenching results of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs in the presence of mandelic acid were evaluated [39] by employing the Stern-Volmer equation [30,84,85], Fig. S4. From Fig. S4, it can be observed that as the temperature increased, the Ksv values decreased. Hence, the results proved that a Meisenheimer complex was formed during the quenching process. It should be mentioned that if the increase in temperature causes a decrease in the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot, the quenching mechanism would be static (i.e., complex formation) [85]. Higher temperatures typically cause the dissociation of weakly bound complexes, and an increase in temperature leads to lower degrees of efficient complexation between L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and mandelic acid molecules [86]. On the other hand, under higher temperatures, the mandelic acid-L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs complex is probably less stable [84].

Moreover, the binding constant as well as the number of binding sites of the analytical probe with the R- and S- enantiomers were investigated as a reliable way [87] for finding the reasons behind the more selective interaction between L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and S-mandelic acid than those of the R-isomer. The values of N (the number of binding sites per CD particle) and K (binding constant) were estimated using log (F0–F)/F = log K + N log [Q] [39] for both enantiomers (Fig. S5) through plotting log (F0–F)/F vs. log [R- or S-Man] which the K and N can be estimated from its intercept and slope, respectively. Based on this evaluation, the binding constants were 2.6 × 10+6 M−1 and 1240.0 M-1 for S- and R-mandelic acid per each L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs particle, respectively. The ratio of KS-Man/KR-man = 2122.75 showed that the interaction between L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and S-mandelic acid was 2123-fold stronger. In addition, the value of the binding site was estimated as 1.31 and 0.83, respectively, for S- and R-mandelic acid. The ratio of the binding site numbers of the enantiomers (NS-Man/NR-Man) was found to be about 1.6. This indicated that S-mandelic acid had more available binding sites which was also a proof of the complex formation, during the quenching process, between mandelic acid enantiomers and L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs. Moreover, the binding constant and site estimation results clearly pronounced the reason for a higher sensitive response of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs toward S-mandelic acid.

To investigate the complexation strength between the edge groups of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and R-as well as S-mandelic acid, the binding Gibbs free energy (ΔG) was calculated using the following formula; ΔG = -RTlnK where R, T, and K are assigned to gas constant (kJ mol−1 K−1), temperature (kelvin), the binding constant (M−1), as reported [39]. Using binding constants of 2.6 × 10+6 M−1 for S- and 1240.0 M-1 for R-mandelic acid, the ΔG values were estimated as −36.65 kJ mol−1 and -17.66 kJ mol−1 (at T = 298.15 K), respectively, for S- and R-mandelic acid. The negative values of ΔG for both S- and R-mandelic acid is assigned to the thermodynamically favorable binding between edge groups of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs and both R- and S-mandelic acid during the non-ionic interaction, as reported [39]. Besides, the more negative value of ΔG for interactions of S-mandelic acid and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs (2-fold more negative than R-mandelic acid) reveals the formation of a more stable complex between S-mandelic acid and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs (i.e., very stronger binding) during the quenching process than R-mandelic acid [39].

Moreover, the FRET efficiency at steady-state conditions (E = 1-F/F0) was calculated and was found to be about 41.2%, and 9.9% for S- and R-mandelic acid, respectively. This indicated that the energy transfer depended on the type of mandelic acid enantiomer [39].

In fact, the binding properties of S- and R-mandelic acid with L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs are attributed to the spatial character of each enantiomer [30,39]. In L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs, the aromatic ring of R-mandelic acid is parallel to the aromatic ring of the chiral selector which is a reason for the steric hindrance. Hence, weaker Pi-Pi interactions between R-mandelic acid and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs develop [88] leading to a weak FL quenching while S-mandelic acid and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs cause a strong FL quenching [39,88]. Besides, the carbonyl group of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs can interact with mandelic acid to form a hydrogen bond which the selectively S-mandelic acid can interact with the carbonyl group of the nanoprobe, causing excellent enantioselective responses of the L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs toward S-mandelic acid with an enantioselectivity coefficient as high as α = 4.17. Based on the above investigations and considerations, the detection mechanism of the developed sensor was schematized in Fig. 3B. As seen in Fig. 3B, both S- and R-Man will have electrostatic interaction with the protonated NH3 + group, and Pi-Pi interaction between the aromatic rings which cause a significant quenching of FL intensity of the developed nanoprobe. However, only S-mandelic acid is able to form a hydrogen bond with the carbonyl group on the modified CDs which is the main reason for the difference in selectivity of the as-prepared L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs toward S- and R-mandelic acid.

To have a rapid, sensitive, and selective chiral determination of mandelic acid, the “one-at-a-time” method was used to optimize various parameters including pH, buffer type, and concentration, as well as the response time. An excitation wavelength of 350.0 nm was used for recording the fluorescence in the wavelength range of 400.0–600.0 nm (λ max = 452.0 nm). The difference between F0/F values of the enantiomers (ΔF0/F) was used as the analytical signal for chiral recognition of 2.0 μM of both S- or R-mandelic acid. The influence of pH was evaluated in the pH range of 2.0–12.0 using citrate buffer with a concentration of 0.05 M. As shown in Fig. S6A, ΔF0/F was higher at pH 6.0 which was then selected as the optimum value. It should be mentioned that at pH = 6.0, mandelic acid has a net unit charge equal to −1 [77] which points to the presence of negatively-charged carboxyl groups in its structure. Hence, mandelic acid acts as an energy donor in the FRET process [39]. Moreover, the amino group of L-try presented on the surface of the analytical probe is protonated in acidic media (see section 3.2) which can accept the energy from negatively-charged mandelic acid [30]. However, the lower response of the sensor at more acidic pHs than pH = 6.0 as well as at basic conditions can be explained by the following discussion Mandelic acid has a hydroxyl group adjacent to its carboxylic group, thus a strong intramolecular H-bond develops between them [78]. As the pH of the detection media increases, the intramolecular H-bond is weakened and eventually disappears [79]; thus, the interactions between mandelic acid and L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs improve and cause a great quenching in FL intensity. At the basic conditions, deprotonation of amino groups of the chiral selector causes the interaction between mandelic acid and the chiral nanoprobe to be weakened.

To study the influence of buffer type, citrate, tris, phosphate, and universal buffer with a concentration of 0.05 M have been examined. Fig. S6B indicates that citrate buffer exhibited the best results; therefore, its concentration was examined in the range of 0.01–0.10 M (Fig. S6C). The results indicated that the highest analytical signal was achieved in 0.05 M citrate buffer (pH 6.0). The effect of response time was checked in the range of 0.0–15.0 min, indicating the best analytical signal was attained after 1.0 min (Fig. S6D).

To measure the analytical parameters, the fluorescence spectra of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs were recorded, under the optimum experimental conditions, in the presence of various concentrations of mandelic acid enantiomers. For both S- and R-mandelic acid enantiomers, the Stern-Volmer plots were constructed. The results are shown in (Fig. 4 A–C). As shown in this figure, two linear working ranges were provided as 0.5–25.0 μM (R2, 0.995) and 0.5–22.0 μM (R2, 0.993) for S- and R-mandelic acid, respectively. Although the fluorescence intensity of L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs was quenched in the presence of both R- and S-enantiomers, the quenching intensity in the presence of S-enantiomers was different from R-enantiomers and calibration sensitivity for S-mandelic acid was found about 5.7-fold higher than that of R-isomer. It should be noted that the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be about 0.047 and 0.26 μM for S- and R-mandelic acid, respectively using LOD = 3Sb/m equation [30], where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank and m is the slope of the calibration curves.

The repeatability and reproducibility as two important indexes of applicability of a sensor were assessed for the developed method toward mandelic acid detection under the optimized experimental conditions. The repeatability of the data (Fig.S7A) was checked by measuring the signals simultaneously from five samples of S-Man (2.0 μM). The RSD percentage was found to be 2.5% which can be deduced that the repeatable L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs-based sensor is an excellent choice for the determination of mandelic acid. Regarding the reproducibility of the sensor, initially, the inter-day validation of the data (Fig.S7B) was performed for the determination of 2.0 μM S-Man solution in three consecutive days using the developed sensor, revealing an RSD percentage of 3.9%. In addition, intra-day validation of the data (Fig.S7C) was checked in three-time intervals (i.e., morning, noon, and evening), the results indicated that there was no considerable change in the measured signals during these processes, and the RSD percentage was found to be about 3.2%. Inter-day and intra-day assays indicated that the L-try–NHCO–SiO2-CDs-based sensor shows very good reproducibility for mandelic acid enantiomers detection.

It is well known that the applicability of a developed sensor is strongly dependent on the selective determination of an analyte in the presence of coexisting materials in real media with a relatively complex matrix. As a common and most important real sample, human blood contains different types of metal ions, amino acids, sugars, and salts which can cause some degree of interference in the measurement of analytical signals in the blood serum. To investigate the effect of possible disturbing species on the mandelic acid quantification in real media, several metal ions presented in the blood including Fe2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, K+, and Na+, and a number of amino acids including L-Ser, L-His, L-Gly, L-Leu, L-Cys, L-Glu, L-Asp, and L-Phe, as well as many selected sugars such as glucose and l-arabinose were tested (Fig. 5 A). The experimental results showed that Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, K+, and Na+ ions had no significant effect on the mandelic acid measurements even up to concentrations 50-times more than mandelic acid. Moreover, Fe2+ ions showed no interference at concentrations 20-fold higher than mandelic acid. Notably, the tested amino acids showed no considerable interruption in mandelic acid determination even at concentration levels ten times higher than mandelic acid. The results also showed that glucose and l-arabinose also had no considerable effect on the analytical signal even at 50- and 20-times higher than mandelic acid, respectively. Overall, the developed sensor exhibited high selectivity for mandelic acid determination in the presence of coexisting materials such as metal ions, sugars, and amino acids.

The ability of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs for the determination of mandelic acid in both synthetic and real samples was checked. Regarding the synthetic samples, the fluorescence spectra of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs were recorded in the presence of a mixture solution of mandelic acid enantiomers with a total molar concentration of 2.0 × 10 −6 mol L−1 and different mole fractions of S-Man. The calibration curve for S-Man in the presence of R-Man was plotted (Fig. 5B). The results clearly showed that the enantiomers of mandelic acid can be recognized from their corresponding calibration graphs.

The performance of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs was examined for S-Man determination in human blood serum samples using the spiked-recovery experiments. The results were obtained in Table 1 . The founded concentrations are in good agreement with the spiking amounts with a recovery percentage over 102.1–104.4%. The results showed that the developed method can be used for the accurate determination of mandelic acid in blood samples.

Herein, amino-functional silica-coated N-doped carbon dots (NH2–SiO2-CDs) were covalently modified by l-tryptophan via producing an amide bond between carboxyl groups of L-try and amino groups of NH2–SiO2-CDs to develop the L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs as novel and high throughput chiral nanoprobes for highly sensitive and enantioselective quantification of S-/R-mandelic acid (S-/R-Man). The chiral determination was performed based on the fluorescence quenching of L-try–CONH–SiO2-CDs by R-/S-Man. The method showed a great difference between S- and R-isomers due to the formation of an ultra-stable Meisenheimer complex between S-isomer (not for R-isomer) and nanoprobe (KS-Man/KR-man = 2122.7, K = binding constant). At optimal conditions, two linear ranges of 0.5–25.0 μM (LOD, 0.05 μM) and 0.5–22.0 μM (LOD, 0.27 μM) for S- and R-Man, respectively, along with an enhanced sensitivity toward S-isomer (i.e. 5.7-fold higher than R-isomer) were achieved. The developed nanosensor exhibited high selectivity for mandelic acid measurement versus some co-existing metal ions, amino acids, and sugars. The mechanistic studies were performed for proving the enantioselective behavior of the developed nanoprobe. It is notable that in terms of figures of merit, the nanosensor was comparable with some reported procedures for the determination of mandelic acid (Table 2 ). Based on this table, the method displayed a very lower detection limit and a very wider dynamic range along with a very lower response time. Moreover, the enantioselectivity coefficient of this method was higher than other methods, showing ultra-enantioselectivity and sensitivity of the developed method was comparable to the reported ones.
